text: If you ask anyone who cooks, there is nothing in the world more frustrating than a blunt knife.
It slows you down, makes you less accurate, makes it more likely you're going to cut yourself.
But did you know that sharpening your knife is something you only need to do about once a month
and will take you no more than five minutes?
What you need to get is a sharpening stone.
And there's two kinds, oil stones and water stones.
If you are buying an oil stone, make sure you get one that's actually made for kitchen knives.
Some of them are very coarse and they're really only intended for garden tools and woodworking tools.
My preference is the thing that chefs love, and that's a water stone.
What you need to look for is the grit number, and the higher the grit number, the smoother the stone,
and that means the more polished result, which means a sharper knife.
Before using a water stone, you need to soak it in water for at least ten minutes.
Now, whatever kind of stone you decide to use, make sure you set it up safely.
And that means putting it on a little piece of damp kitchen paper to prevent it from sliding around.
First and foremost, hold the knife securely.
So use a pinch grip at the bottom of the blade, three fingers around the handle,
and then tap it.
You can stabilize the blade with the fingers of your other hand.
Then it's a matter of swinging from the shoulders, never from the elbows,
scraping backwards and forwards about a dozen times.
Then reverse your position and do the other side.
You're going to need about six or seven sets of that in order to create a good edge.
The most important thing to do is to keep that angle constant at all times.
But it's not quite there yet.
We've honed it, we haven't polished it.
For that, you need a steel.
You might think steels are for sharpening, but they're not.
They're only good for getting rid of the burrs on the edge of the blade
and also for polishing the metal so it'll slide through things more smoothly.
All you do is slide on the same angle, maybe a dozen or 15 times on each side,
and your knife's ready to go.
That's a knife.
